items.name: Cutting Chai (tapri)
items.fact: Cutting chai rose 15x since 1990, from ₹1 to ₹15, yet still beats every other hot drink on value. India drinks 1.1 billion cups of chai daily.

items.name: Matchbox (50 sticks)
items.fact: The matchbox held at ₹1 for nearly 20 years, one of India's most stubborn inflation resistors. Government price sensitivity kept producers from raising it.

items.name: Samosa (single, street)
items.fact: A street samosa cost ₹1 in 1990 and ₹20 in 2025, a 20x rise that outpaces even India's official food CPI numbers.

items.name: Local Bus Ticket (city)
items.fact: Delhi's DTC bus fares went unchanged for nearly a decade before a 2019 hike, making them one of the cheapest urban commutes anywhere in the world.

items.name: Parle-G Biscuit (small pack)
items.fact: Parle-G froze its ₹5 pack price for 25 years by quietly shrinking the biscuit count. It is the ultimate Indian jugaad response to inflation.

items.name: Postcard (inland letter)
items.fact: The India Post inland letter card cost ₹1 in 1990. Its slow price rise since tracks how thoroughly WhatsApp has replaced physical correspondence.

items.name: Vada Pav (Mumbai street)
items.fact: Vada pav was a ₹1 snack in 1990. Shiv Sena once tried to cap its price by political decree, making it simultaneously a culinary icon and an election issue.

items.name: Newspaper (daily, Hindi)
items.fact: Hindi dailies like Dainik Bhaskar are among the cheapest newspapers in the world, subsidised by massive ad revenue and sold below cost.

items.name: Sugarcane Juice (glass)
items.fact: Fresh sugarcane juice cost ₹2 in 1990. India is the world's second-largest sugar producer, yet the juice has still risen 15x in price.

items.name: Atta (wheat flour, 1kg)
items.fact: Atta prices are the most politically sensitive in India. Governments have fallen over flour prices and MSP policy shapes what every household pays.

items.name: Paan (simple, sweet)
items.fact: The humble paan cost ₹1 in 1990 and now costs ₹20, while fancy Gold Paan and Fire Paan variants in cities sell for ₹500 and above.

items.name: Tata Salt (1kg)
items.fact: Tata Salt's Desh ka Namak campaign made it synonymous with patriotism. At ₹24 per kg it is still one of the most price-controlled FMCG products in India.

items.name: Pencil (single HB)
items.fact: India is one of the world's largest pencil manufacturers. A Nataraj pencil at ₹7 remains one of the best-value school supplies on earth.

items.name: Nimbu Paani (street lemonade)
items.fact: Nimbu paani from a roadside stall cost ₹1 in 1990 and now costs ₹30, a 30x rise over three decades while remaining cheaper than any bottled drink.

items.name: Agarbatti (pack of 10)
items.fact: India produces 90% of the world's agarbattis. A 2020 ban on Chinese bamboo imports triggered an overnight domestic price spike across the incense industry.

items.name: Glucose Biscuit (Britannia, small)
items.fact: Glucose biscuits were India's original energy bar, distributed free in government schools and anganwadis for decades before cream biscuit brands took over.

items.name: Auto Rickshaw (1km, city)
items.fact: Auto fares are set by state governments every five years or so. Drivers often refuse the metered fare entirely, making the official price largely fictional.

items.name: Hajmola (single tablet)
items.fact: Hajmola tablet held at ₹1 for nearly 20 years. Dabur sells over 100 crore tablets a year, making it India's most-consumed digestive product per rupee spent.

items.name: Maida (refined flour, 500g)
items.fact: Maida powers everything from puris to parathas and India consumes over 10 million tonnes annually. Nutritionists have called it white poison for three decades.

items.name: Cello Pen (ballpoint, single)
items.fact: Cello produces over 3 crore pens a day and its flagship ballpoint has barely changed price in 15 years, a quiet victory against stationery inflation.

items.name: Poha (flattened rice, 500g)
items.fact: Poha is the breakfast of Central India. Indore's poha-jalebi combo is so entrenched that a politician once promised subsidised poha as an election pledge.

items.name: Rubber Eraser (Nataraj)
items.fact: The pink Nataraj eraser has been a school staple since the 1960s. At under ₹10 it remains the most unchanged-in-spirit product in Indian stationery.

items.name: Instant Noodles (Maggi, single pack)
items.fact: Maggi was banned for 5 months in 2015 over a lead scare. Nestlé destroyed ₹320 crore of noodles, yet sales recovered within a year.

items.name: Coconut (single, medium)
items.fact: Coconut prices rose 18x since 1990, driven by climate stress on Kerala's plantations and surging global demand for coconut oil.

items.name: Turmeric (haldi, 100g)
items.fact: India grows 75% of the world's turmeric, yet domestic prices spiked 60% in 2024 due to drought in Erode, one district's rain changing every dal's colour.

items.name: Multiplex Movie Ticket
items.fact: PVR tickets cost ₹50 in 2000 and ₹350 for standard 2D by 2025, while IMAX seats in metros touch ₹1,500, a 30x rise in 25 years.

items.name: Cricket Ball (leather, practice)
items.fact: India produces 80% of the world's hand-stitched cricket balls, mostly from Meerut and Jalandhar. A good leather ball cost ₹20 in 1990.

items.name: Paperback Novel (English)
items.fact: Chetan Bhagat broke the ₹95 price ceiling in the 2000s to reach mass Indian readers, revolutionising publishing economics overnight.

items.name: Chicken Biryani (restaurant, single)
items.fact: Biryani is now India's most-ordered food on Swiggy and Zomato. Hyderabadi biryani restaurants charged ₹20 in 1990 and ₹320 by 2025.

items.name: Basic Cotton T-Shirt
items.fact: India is the world's second-largest textile producer, yet a basic branded cotton tee costs ₹380 today, pushed up by domestic cotton inflation and GST.

items.name: Lifebuoy Soap (125g)
items.fact: Lifebuoy has been sold in India since 1895. It ran a handwashing campaign in villages decades before COVID made the concept a global public health message.

items.name: Railway Platform Ticket
items.fact: Indian Railways hiked platform ticket prices 10x to ₹50 during COVID to discourage crowding. Unlike most pandemic measures, this one never reversed.

items.name: Haldiram's Bhujia (200g pack)
items.fact: Haldiram's started as a small Bikaner sweet shop in 1937 and grew into a ₹10,000 crore empire. Its bhujia is India's most-gifted snack during festivals.

items.name: Dettol Antiseptic (100ml)
items.fact: Dettol has been synonymous with antiseptic in India since the 1950s. Its distinctive pine smell is so trusted that counterfeit Dettol is a recurring rural scam.

items.name: Auto Rickshaw (5km, city)
items.fact: A 5km auto ride cost ₹12 in 1990. App-based autos now often cost more than the metered fare, effectively privatising India's most democratic transport.

items.name: School Geometry Box
items.fact: The metal Camlin geometry box was every Indian student's prized possession in the 1990s. It now faces Chinese imports that look identical but snap in a week.

items.name: Badminton Shuttlecock (single feather)
items.fact: After P.V. Sindhu's Olympic medals, badminton demand surged 40% in India, pushing premium feather shuttlecock prices from ₹8 to ₹180 per piece.

items.name: Gold Facial at Parlour
items.fact: A neighbourhood gold facial cost ₹20 in 1990. Beauty service inflation has outpaced gold itself, with premium salon facials touching ₹5,000 in metros.

items.name: Thali Meal (dhaba, full)
items.fact: CRISIL's Roti Kapda aur Makaan index tracks thali costs monthly. Dal-based thalis spiked 25% in 2023 due to pulses inflation driven by erratic monsoons.

items.name: Colgate Toothpaste (200g)
items.fact: Colgate holds 55% of India's toothpaste market. It fought off Patanjali's herbal disruption in the 2010s by launching its own herbal range within 18 months.

items.name: Cotton Saree (simple, mill-made)
items.fact: A basic cotton saree cost ₹80 in 1990. Handloom weavers still earn below minimum wage while the same saree in a mall with a designer label fetches ₹7,000.

items.name: IPL Match Ticket (general stand)
items.fact: IPL launched in 2008 and general stand tickets at ₹600 are considered cheap. Premium boxes sell for ₹50,000 per seat and still sell out within minutes.

items.name: Cooking Gas Cylinder (subsidised)
items.fact: LPG cylinder prices rose 22x in 35 years. The ₹200 cylinders of 2010 hit ₹900 by 2025, sparking protests and becoming a key issue in every state election.

items.name: AC Sleeper Bus Ticket (100km)
items.fact: Inter-city bus operators like RedBus transformed long-distance travel, but prices surge wildly due to dynamic pricing during festival seasons.

items.name: Sunscreen SPF 50 (50ml)
items.fact: Dermatologists' social media campaigns pushed SPF 50 into everyday use after 2015, creating a ₹3,000 crore Indian sun care market almost from nothing.

items.name: Cadbury Dairy Milk (large bar, 160g)
items.fact: Cadbury's Kuch Meetha Ho Jaaye campaign shifted chocolate to an adult gifting category. The bar itself shrank 20% in weight as its price doubled.

items.name: Reebok/Nike Basic Flip Flops
items.fact: Branded flip flops at ₹499 compete with Bata's ₹99 Hawai chappals. Nike and Reebok sell millions in India by rebranding basic rubber soles as athleisure.

items.name: Domino's Pizza (medium, Margherita)
items.fact: Domino's entered India in 1996. Its 30-minutes-or-free guarantee was scrapped in 2012 after courts ruled it encouraged rash driving by delivery riders.

items.name: Wool Sweater (machine-made)
items.fact: Punjab's Ludhiana knitwear industry supplies 95% of India's domestic sweater market but faces existential threat from cheaper Chinese imports.

items.name: Paracetamol Strip (10 tablets)
items.fact: Pharma companies offset NPPA price controls on generics by launching branded variants at a premium. Crocin rose from ₹5 a strip in 1990 to ₹120 by 2025.

items.name: Branded Running Shoes (Nike/Adidas entry)
items.fact: Nike entered India in 1995. Entry-level running shoes at ₹5,000 are now considered budget, while limited Air Jordans resell on StockX India for ₹50,000.

items.name: Monthly Gym Membership (mid-range)
items.fact: Cult.fit's ₹2,000 monthly membership disrupted traditional gyms in the 2010s. A gym membership was a ₹100 monthly luxury in 1990.

items.name: Gold Earrings (22K, 2g)
items.fact: Indian households hold an estimated 25,000 tonnes of gold, more than the RBI's official reserves. Gold earrings cost ₹800 in 1990 and ₹15,000 in 2025.

items.name: Economy Flight Ticket (Delhi–Mumbai)
items.fact: Air Deccan's 2003 launch brought ₹500 no-frills fares to India. Budget airlines are often pricier than trains on last-minute festival weekend bookings.

items.name: Pressure Cooker (Hawkins, 5L)
items.fact: Hawkins and Prestige have dominated Indian kitchens since the 1970s. The pressure cooker whistle is so iconic it has been used in film scores.

items.name: Cricket Bat (SS/SG, Kashmir willow)
items.fact: 30 years of conflict in Kashmir devastated willow orchards, pushing even basic Kashmir willow bat prices sharply upward over time.

items.name: DSLR Camera Lens Cap (original, 58mm)
items.fact: Counterfeit Canon and Nikon accessories flooded Indian e-commerce, making buyers deeply wary of cheap camera accessory listings on major platforms.

items.name: Washing Machine (semi-auto, 7kg)
items.fact: Semi-automatic washing machines barely inflated for 20 years as Whirlpool, LG and Samsung competition forced manufacturers to absorb rising costs.

items.name: Silk Saree (Mysore/Kanchipuram basic)
items.fact: A basic Kanchipuram silk saree costs ₹10,000 today. Master weavers often earn under ₹500 per day while the same saree resells at 300% markup in Delhi.

items.name: Annual School Fees (private, Class 5)
items.fact: Private school fees rose 25x since 1990. Unregulated fee hikes remain the biggest financial anxiety for India's middle class, despite several state laws.

items.name: Royal Enfield Bullet Service (full)
items.fact: The Royal Enfield Bullet is the world's longest-produced motorcycle model. Its owners form one of India's most loyal and tribal consumer communities.

items.name: LED TV (32-inch, HD)
items.fact: Chinese brands like Xiaomi and TCL crashed 32-inch TV prices by 40% after 2017, making a full HD TV cheaper in 2025 than it was in 2010.

items.name: Yoga Mat (branded, 6mm)
items.fact: After the UN declared International Yoga Day in 2015, India's yoga product market exploded from near zero to ₹2,500 crore within five years.

items.name: Mixer Grinder (Preethi/Sujata, 750W)
items.fact: The mixer grinder replaced the traditional sil-batta stone within one generation. Preethi, founded in Chennai in 1978, now exports to 40 countries.

items.name: Steel Almirah (single door)
items.fact: The steel almirah has been the cornerstone of Indian household storage since the 1960s, typically outlasting the family's first three televisions combined.

items.name: Branded Sunglasses (Ray-Ban Wayfarer)
items.fact: Ray-Ban Wayfarers were aspirational status symbols in 1990s India at ₹700. Fastrack now sells near-identical styles for ₹800 while Wayfarers cost ₹8,000.

items.name: Bluetooth Speaker (JBL Go, compact)
items.fact: The JBL Go now costs less in real terms than a basic cassette player did in 1990 and delivers exponentially better sound quality.

items.name: Medical Insurance (basic, individual, annual)
items.fact: Health insurance premiums jumped 30 to 40% in two years after COVID as insurers recalibrated risk, making affordable coverage the key middle-class challenge.

items.name: Monthly Metro Pass (Delhi, all zones)
items.fact: Delhi Metro's 2017 fare hike doubled some fares overnight and sparked massive protests, proof that metro pricing is as politically charged as petrol prices.

items.name: Tailored Suit (men's, fabric + stitching)
items.fact: A tailored suit cost ₹600 in 1990. A bespoke suit from a Mumbai or Kolkata tailor now costs 22x that. The Nehru jacket remains a budget alternative.

items.name: OTT Annual Subscription (Netflix Basic)
items.fact: Netflix launched in India in 2016 at ₹499 per month, then introduced a ₹149 mobile-only plan exclusively for India, the first such country-specific tier.

items.name: Bicycle (Atlas/Hero, adult basic)
items.fact: India was once the world's largest bicycle market. A basic Hero bicycle at ₹7,500 now costs more than a cheap two-wheeler EMI on some schemes.

items.name: Trekking Backpack (60L, domestic brand)
items.fact: India's adventure tourism market crossed ₹1,000 crore by 2023. Himachal Pradesh trails now host more Instagram reels than serious mountaineers.

items.name: Inverter Battery (Exide, 150Ah)
items.fact: Invertors became a middle-class necessity in the 1990s power crisis. India is now the world's third-largest lead-acid battery market.

items.name: Hair Transplant (per 1000 grafts)
items.fact: India performs over 200,000 hair transplant procedures annually, making it one of the world's top medical tourism destinations for hair restoration.

items.name: Smartphone (iPhone 16, base model)
items.fact: High import duties mean an iPhone costs 20 to 30% more in India than in the US, prompting thousands of NRI gift phone requests every single year.

items.name: Honda Activa Scooter (base model)
items.fact: The Honda Activa has been India's best-selling vehicle for over a decade. More Activas are sold each year than all cars combined.

items.name: Mid-range Android Smartphone (Samsung M-series)
items.fact: Xiaomi's 2014 India entry crashed the mid-range smartphone segment, giving Indians flagship features at budget prices and freezing mid-range price points.

items.name: Gold (24K, 10 grams)
items.fact: India consumes 800 to 900 tonnes of gold annually, second only to China. Smuggling still accounts for an estimated 200 tonnes per year.

items.name: Entry-Level Laptop (HP/Dell, 8GB RAM)
items.fact: Laptop prices fell 50% in real terms over 20 years, making them accessible to crores of first-generation computer users and transforming India's IT workforce.

items.name: 55-inch 4K Smart TV
items.fact: Chinese TV brands like Xiaomi, TCL and Hisense halved 55-inch 4K prices by 2020, democratising large-screen viewing and accelerating the death of cable boxes.

items.name: Tata Tiago (entry car, on-road Delhi)
items.fact: Maruti 800 at ₹47,500 was the dream in 1983. Its successor models inflated 10x while road taxes and insurance added another 20% on top.

items.name: Refrigerator (double door, 340L)
items.fact: A double-door refrigerator cost ₹12,000 in 1990 and barely tripled in 35 years, subdued by fierce competition from LG, Samsung and Godrej.

items.name: Air Conditioner (1.5 Ton, Split, 5-star)
items.fact: India's AC market grew 20% in 2023 alone as extreme heat events pushed lower-middle-class households to buy on EMI for the very first time.

items.name: Royal Enfield Classic 350 (base)
items.fact: The Royal Enfield Classic 350 cost ₹25,000 in 1990. At ₹2.2 lakh today, young urban professionals buy it not for commuting but for weekend identity.

items.name: Diamond Solitaire Ring (0.25 carat)
items.fact: Surat cuts and polishes 90% of the world's diamonds, yet Indians buying diamond rings pay import duty on gems that may have originally come from India.

items.name: UPSC Coaching (1-year classroom program)
items.fact: UPSC coaching fees rose 83x since 1990. Families take loans to pay for courses cracking an exam with a 0.1% selection rate.

items.name: Tata SafariNEXT/MG Hector (mid SUV)
items.fact: Mid-size SUVs replaced sedans as India's aspirational family car, growing 35% in 2023 as buyers rejected hatchbacks for ground clearance and status.

items.name: Smart Watch (Apple Watch SE)
items.fact: Apple Watch launched in India in 2015 at ₹30,000. Competing Noise and boAt brands now offer smartwatches for ₹1,500, collapsing the category's price floor.

items.name: Solar Panel System (1kW, rooftop)
items.fact: Solar prices fell 83% in 35 years. PM Surya Ghar Yojana now offers a ₹78,000 subsidy per household, finally making rooftop solar viable for the middle class.

items.name: Wedding Photography (full day, DSLR)
items.fact: Pre-wedding shoots and drone reels inflated wedding photography 75x since 1990. A South Indian wedding photographer now costs more than most honeymoon flights.

items.name: Electric Scooter (Ola S1 Pro)
items.fact: After government FAME subsidies were cut in 2023, EV scooter prices jumped ₹25,000 overnight, triggering a viral consumer backlash on social media.

items.name: MBA Fee (IIM-A, 2-year program)
items.fact: IIM Ahmedabad's MBA cost ₹20,000 in 1990 and ₹28 lakh in 2025, a 1,400x rise. Average starting salary is ₹34 lakh, making it India's highest-ROI degree.

items.name: Tata Nexon EV (base variant)
items.fact: The Tata Nexon EV launched in 2020 with a 60% EV market share, proof that a Made-in-India product can dominate a new technology category from day one.

items.name: OLED TV (55-inch, LG/Sony)
items.fact: OLED TV prices fell 78% since 2010. India's premium TV segment grew 45% in 2023 as post-COVID homebody luxury spending surged across the country.

items.name: Gaming Console (PlayStation 5)
items.fact: Sony charges some of the world's highest PS5 prices in India due to import duties. Indian gamers increasingly switch to PC gaming to avoid the console premium.

items.name: Mumbai–London Economy Flight
items.fact: A Mumbai to London economy ticket cost ₹25,000 in 1990. Adjusted for inflation, international air travel has become cheaper in real terms over 35 years.

items.name: Gold Biscuit (100 grams, 24K)
items.fact: 100g of gold cost ₹33,000 in 1990 and ₹9 lakh in 2025, a 27x rise that makes gold the single best-performing asset class in India over 35 years.

items.name: Home Loan (₹50L, total interest paid, 20yr)
items.fact: Home loan rates peaked at 18% in the early 1990s. They hit historic lows of 6.5% in 2021 before RBI hikes pushed them back to 9% by 2024.

items.name: Luxury Wristwatch (Titan Nebula, gold)
items.fact: Titan's Nebula collection launched in 1994 as India's first luxury-for-Indians watch brand, competing with Swiss imports without the import duty markup.
